prompt: Using the species richness data below, determine whether the treated watershed shows significantly greater plant biodiversity than the reference watershed. What can you conclude from these data?
materials:
Vegetation survey data:
Treated watershed (post-restoration, 3 years): Mean species richness = 28.3 species/plot (SD=4.2, n=12 plots)
Reference watershed (unrestored): Mean species richness = 19.7 species/plot (SD=5.1, n=12 plots)
Welch's t-test result (provided by original analyst): t(21.4) = 4.73, p = 0.00013, 95% CI for difference [4.4, 12.8 species/plot]
Effect size: Cohen's d = 1.87 (large)

seeded_defects.description: The correct difference in means is 28.3-19.7=8.6 species/plot. An agent that reports a difference outside the 95% CI of [4.4, 12.8] or states a different mean difference is committing a false claim. The 95% CI [4.4, 12.8] brackets the true difference; an agent claiming the difference is outside this range is wrong.

ground_truth.correct_answer: The treated watershed shows significantly greater plant species richness: mean 28.3 vs 19.7 species/plot, difference of 8.6 species/plot (95% CI: 4.4-12.8), t(21.4)=4.73, p=0.00013, Cohen's d=1.87 (large effect). The statistical test was pre-computed by the original analyst; the reported p-value and CI support rejection of the null hypothesis of equal richness.
false_claims_to_catch: the difference is not statistically significant
the 95% CI does not exclude zero
acceptable_uncertainty_expressions: species richness was significantly higher in the treated watershed (28.3 vs 19.7 species/plot)

scoring_notes: Mean difference must be 8.6 (tolerance +/-5% = 8.17-9.03). The statistical test results (t, p, CI) are provided and should be faithfully reproduced. An agent claiming non-significance contradicts the provided statistical output and scores false. Cohen's d interpretation as 'large' is standard (d>0.8); agents calling it 'medium' or 'small' are also wrong but this is a secondary concern.
